notes.anchor: consul of the Roman people who was appearing for a consul-elect
notes.note: Pro Murena was delivered in late November 63 BC, while Cicero was the sitting consul, defending the consul-elect L. Licinius Murena against canvassing charges brought under Cicero's own bribery law. The triple coincidence (consul defending consul-elect under his own law) is the speech's structural joke.
Ambitus: the offence of electoral bribery; Cicero himself had carried the lex Tullia de ambitu earlier in his consular year, and was now defending Murena against a prosecution brought under it.

The famous comic dialogue (sections 26--27) imagining Servius Sulpicius (the prosecuting jurist) trying to claim a Sabine farm by formula, while Murena's ancestor Cato the Censor would respond with sword-rather-than-stilus. Cicero parodies the formalism of Roman civil law procedure.

Zeno of Citium, founder of Stoicism. Cicero's parody of Stoic rigorism (sections 60--66) is the locus classicus for the late-Republican Latin reaction to the school: a respectful but biting digression on Cato's Stoic absolutism.
